topic: Section 1.9: Differential Refraction — The Eclipse Geometry Trap (EXP-144 integration)
our_argument_summary: Section 1.9 argues that the dome's aetheric refraction fix (introduced for the sun in Section 1.5/1.8) creates an unresolved differential-refraction problem for the moon, making eclipse geometry incomputable. The medium n(r) = 1 + 0.20 × (8537/H(r) − 1) reaches n=1.44 at the equator (r≈10,008 km) and n=2.84 at the ice wall (r=20,015 km) — comparable to glass. Snell's law applies to all light, so moonlight is equally refracted. The dome has never computed (a) the moon's physical altitude under n(r) (its 2,534 km figure is necessarily apparent), (b) the bent shadow-cone path through n(r), or (c) the angular-diameter ratio under differential refraction. The dome therefore stakes nine eclipse-related WINs and an August 2026 falsification test on geometry it cannot compute. The section preempts the most likely defender response (refraction is sun-only).

dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: N/A — section is structural/physical argument, not code-based.
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: Dome model page text mentions 'optical illusion due to aetheric refraction' for the sun only — never quantifies moon refraction; never lists OPEN problem for moon-medium interaction.
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: Section 1.9 does not make code-level claims; it argues from the dome's published architecture (sun apparent 5,733 km, sun physical < 4,200 km, moon altitude 2,534 km, H(r) and n(r) functions). All dome-side numbers are correctly quoted from raw-text/05-model.txt.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: Section-level review — no per-WIN monitoring tag applies. Section makes structural argument about refraction physics, not about a single observation/prediction comparison.
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: Argument is original to our review (differential refraction → incomputable eclipse geometry). Standard physics handles refractive media correctly; the dome's selective application is the issue.
code_analysis_tags.post_hoc_evidence: Argument is constructed from first principles applied to the dome's published n(r) inference. Not a measurement-vs-prediction comparison.
code_analysis_tags.derives_from_dome_evidence: All numerical claims (n=1.44 at equator, n=2.84 at ice wall, sun gap ≥1,500 km) are derived from the dome's published H(r) and the n(r) ansatz our Section 1.5 inferred from the dome's distance-correction formula d_measured = d_geometric / n(r_avg).

holes_found.description: CROSS-SECTION INCONSISTENCY (Section 1.5 vs 1.9 vs 1.8): Section 1.9 says "At the equator (r ≈ 10,008 km), n = 1.44." Section 1.8 also says "1.44 at the equator." But Section 1.5 STILL says "at the equatorial ring (r = 14,105 km), n ≈ 1.83" — the OLD value that ISS-855 was supposed to retire. Section 1.5 ALSO contains the new "1.44 at the equator" figure later in its own prose (after the n(r) formula derivation). So a reader who follows our cross-reference from Section 1.9 → Section 1.5 ("see Section 1.5 for why the refraction fix cannot be abandoned") immediately encounters a contradiction: Section 1.5 says equator-r is BOTH 14,105 km (with n=1.83) AND somewhere implying n=1.44. A dome defender will quote our own pages back at us: 'Your refraction critique can't agree on basic n(r) values across adjacent sections, why should we trust the rest?' The ISS-855 patch was applied to the new Section 1.9 but NOT propagated to the existing Section 1.5 prose. EXP-144's own holes_addressed claims it 'removed incorrect r=14,105 km equatorial ring label and wrong n=1.40/3.49 values' — but only inside the EXP package; the live Section 1.5 was never edited. The newly integrated SEC-1.9 is internally consistent, but its publication exposes the unfixed legacy text in SEC-1.5.
holes_found.recommendation: File a follow-up issue: patch Section 1.5 to remove the stale "r = 14,105 km, n ≈ 1.83" sentence and replace with the corrected equator value (r ≈ 10,008 km, n = 1.44). Alternative: keep the r=14,105 figure but relabel it accurately (e.g., "the southern reflection radius" or whatever it actually represents) and explain its physical meaning. Either way, Section 1.5 must not contradict Sections 1.8/1.9 about where the equator is on the disc.

holes_found.description: DEFENDER ESCAPE NOT ADDRESSED — APPARENT-PATH SUFFICIENCY FOR GROUND EFFECTS: The section's central claim is that eclipse magnetic/gravity/ionospheric effects (WIN-010, WIN-025, WIN-068) require knowing the *physical* shadow path, not the *apparent* alignment. But these effects are caused by photon flux loss AT THE GROUND OBSERVATORY. The fraction of sunlight blocked at observer X is a directly observable quantity (eclipse magnitude reported in NASA catalogs, etc.). To predict the magnetic dip at observer X during eclipse Y, the dome only needs the observed eclipse magnitude at X — which is a measured input, not a derived quantity. The dome can therefore use NASA's apparent eclipse path data without ever needing to know where the moon "physically" is. The section's preempted objection #1 ("Apparent alignment is sufficient") tries to address this with "these effects depend on where photons are physically blocked (the shadow cone), not on optical appearance" — but this conflates *physical photon-blockage at the observer's position* (which IS the apparent eclipse magnitude as seen from that position) with *knowing the moon's physical location in 3-space* (which is irrelevant for predicting per-station ground effects). A smart defender will say: "We don't compute shadow geometry from first principles — we read NASA's eclipse-path data and predict per-station magnetic responses. Whether the moon is at altitude 2,534 km or 1,200 km doesn't change what magnetometers measure during the eclipse." This escape valve weakens the section's claim that the WINs rest on "geometry the model cannot compute."
holes_found.recommendation: Strengthen the argument by distinguishing two cases: (a) WINs whose predictions are scaled *across stations* using the FSF-style station-distance formula — these DO require the shadow-cone geometry (axis, width, ground intersection path) to scale per-station amplitudes correctly. WIN-025 (9-station) and WIN-068 (39-eclipse ensemble) use such scaling. Without the bent-shadow path, the dome's per-station scaling is wrong even when per-station eclipse magnitudes are observationally fixed. (b) WINs whose predictions are single-observatory (WIN-010 BOU only) — for these, the apparent-magnitude argument largely escapes the differential-refraction objection. The section currently treats all eclipse WINs identically; sharpening this distinction makes the structural argument more defensible against the apparent-path-sufficiency counter.

holes_found.description: n(r) IS RADIAL (DISC POSITION), NOT ALTITUDINAL: The section states "the moon orbits at roughly r ≈ 10,000 km, where n ≈ 1.44." Here r is the moon's *radial position over the disc* (its horizontal distance from the pole). The dome's n(r) formula gives the refractive index *at every point in the cavity at radial position r*, regardless of altitude z. This means n is constant in the vertical direction at fixed r (the medium varies horizontally, not vertically). For light traveling nearly vertically (moon nearly overhead), Snell's law in a medium with no vertical gradient produces zero vertical bending — only horizontal displacement as the ray crosses changing n regions. The section's argument that moonlight "is shifted from its physical position by an amount the dome has never calculated" is true for grazing-elevation observations but weakens for near-overhead observations. A dome defender could argue: "For the equatorial observer with moon overhead, vertical light path through a horizontally-uniform-locally medium is essentially unbent; only oblique-elevation observations see significant refraction." The section doesn't anticipate this geometric subtlety. Note: this is a real physics issue, not an artifact — n(r) as a purely radial function is an unusual ansatz, and our Section 1.5 should arguably critique that ansatz rather than accept it as the dome's intended model.
holes_found.recommendation: Add a paragraph acknowledging that n(r) as a radial-only function produces refraction primarily for oblique/grazing rays — but use this to STRENGTHEN the eclipse argument: eclipse paths cross thousands of km of horizontal n(r) variation, so per-station refraction differences along a transcontinental shadow path are large even if the local vertical bending is small. The shadow-axis tilt accumulates with horizontal distance through varying n. This actually HARDENS the per-station scaling problem (hole #2) for WIN-025/WIN-068.

holes_found.description: ANGULAR-DIAMETER ARGUMENT IS DIRECTIONALLY UNCONSTRAINED: The section says "If differential refraction shrinks the moon's apparent size relative to the sun's, total eclipses become impossible." The argument is hedged with "If" but its rhetorical force depends on the shrinking direction. Refraction through an n(r) gradient could in principle either magnify or shrink apparent angular sizes, depending on the geometry. Without computing the sign of the effect, this is more of a "the dome hasn't checked" point than a positive prediction of failure. A defender could counter: "Refraction could equally enlarge the moon's apparent size — possibly explaining why total eclipses occur at all in the dome model." This counter doesn't actually rescue the dome (you'd need to compute it to know), but it dilutes the section's framing of "total eclipses become impossible."
holes_found.recommendation: Reframe as: "Without computation, the dome cannot say whether n(r) preserves, enlarges, or shrinks the angular-diameter ratio. The fact that we observe both annular and total eclipses tightly constrains the moon-sun apparent-diameter ratio to ≈ 1.0, with millisecond-precision agreement of predicted vs observed eclipse durations. The dome would need to demonstrate that its n(r) preserves this ratio over centuries of eclipse observations — a derivation it has never attempted." This is more defensible than the speculative "shrinks → annular only" framing.

holes_found.description: OVERSTATED "DOMINATES ALL LIGHT PROPAGATION" CLAIM: The section's bottom line says n ranges "1.08 to 2.84" and concludes the medium "dominates all light propagation in the cavity." The 1.08 floor at r ≈ 3,000 km is correct, but for the broad pole-proximate region (r < 3,000 km, covering most of the densely-populated northern hemisphere on the dome map), n is between 1.0 and 1.08 — i.e., the medium is comparable to or weaker than typical glass-air interfaces in optical terms. Northern-hemisphere observatories (most of the eclipse magnetic data) operate in a region where the n(r) effect is measurably small. The "dominates all light propagation" framing is true for the southern hemisphere and the ice wall but overstated for the populated regions where most magnetic observations occur. A defender could exploit this by saying: "The eclipse magnetic data we predict comes from northern observatories where your own n(r) formula gives n ≈ 1.0–1.1. Your refraction critique applies primarily to the southern dome, not to where the data is collected."
holes_found.recommendation: Add a sentence acknowledging that n grows with r — northern observatories see modest refraction, but the SHADOW PATH crosses the entire range of n values as it transits across the disc. The per-station scaling problem (hole #2) holds even when individual stations see modest local n, because the ray geometry between sun, moon, and station traverses the full n(r) range across the disc.

holes_found.description: WIN-039 LISTED IN AFFECTED_WINS BUT NOT REFERENCED IN PROSE: EXP-144 lists WIN-039 (Lunar magnetic 1-2 nT) among affected_wins ("coupling strength depends on physical moon distance"), but the integrated section text never names WIN-039. The lunar-magnetic argument is a strong addition to the differential-refraction case (any inverse-square coupling depends on the moon's actual distance, not its apparent altitude). Excluding it from the prose loses an argument the analyst correctly identified.
holes_found.recommendation: Add a one-sentence reference to WIN-039 in the "Why this matters for eclipses" or "bottom line" paragraph: "The same problem affects WIN-039 (lunar magnetic 1–2 nT coupling) — any distance-dependent coupling formula requires the moon's PHYSICAL distance, which is unknown under n(r)."

holes_found.description: INTERNAL JUSTIFICATION ERROR IN EXP-144 (NOT PUBLISHED): EXP-144's anticipated_objections response #3 says "At the moon's orbit, n=1.44 — 4,800× Earth's atmospheric refraction (n=1.0003)." Recompute: (1.44 − 1)/(1.0003 − 1) = 0.44/0.0003 = 1,467×. The 4,800× figure is wrong by a factor of ~3.3. Note: this number is NOT in the published Section 1.9 prose (the section says "Earth's atmosphere has n ≈ 1.0003" without the multiplier), so this is not a published error. But it indicates the analyst's supporting reasoning had a numerical slip. Section 1.8 separately uses "300 to 6,000 times more refractive" which is correct (300× at r≈3,200, 6,000× at the ice wall). The published prose is fine; only the EXP justification material has the slip.
holes_found.recommendation: Correct future EXP packages: (n − 1)/(n_atm − 1) is the correct refraction-strength ratio, not n_dome/n_atm or any other naive ratio. Internal review materials should be numerically clean even when not destined for publication.

holes_found.description: TLDR SLIGHTLY OVERSTATES CERTAINTY: TLDR says "eclipse alignment — which requires physical overlap, not just optical coincidence — becomes indeterminate." The "which requires physical overlap" clause is the disputed premise (see hole #2). A dome defender will say "actually, ground magnetic effects depend on where the OBSERVER experiences darkness, which IS optical coincidence at the observer's location." The TLDR baldly asserts the disputed claim as fact. Per the curmudgeon TLDR rules: "Don't split hairs. The TLDR is for a non-science reader — it simplifies by design. Only flag if the simplification is actually wrong, not just imprecise." This sits at the borderline — it's not technically wrong (per-station scaling really does require shadow-cone geometry per hole #2), but a non-science reader may take "physical overlap not optical coincidence" too literally. Severity minor (not major) because the underlying argument can be sharpened to support the TLDR rather than the TLDR being factually wrong.
holes_found.recommendation: Consider softening to: "Eclipse magnetic and gravity predictions depend on shadow geometry the dome cannot compute under its own refraction model." This is true regardless of whether per-station effects could be predicted from observed eclipse magnitudes (because shadow scaling formulas — see WIN-025 FSF — definitely require the shadow-cone path).

stronger_arguments.description: FALSIFIABLE TEST: The differential-refraction argument can be sharpened into a falsifiable prediction the dome must answer. The dome's August 2026 eclipse prediction (PRED in eclipse category, test_window 2026-08, 14 stations) gives per-station magnetic dips. If the dome computed those without bent-shadow ray-tracing, the dome must EITHER: (a) publish its derivation showing it accounts for n(r) in the per-station scaling, OR (b) admit it used straight-line geometry (contradicting the refraction fix). This binary creates a clean public falsification path: ask the dome to publish its 2026 calculation procedure BEFORE August. Either it accounts for n(r) (huge new derivation, never seen), or it admits the contradiction. Section 1.9 should explicitly issue this challenge as part of the section's bottom line.
sources: data/predictions.json eclipse PRED entries with August 2026 windows

stronger_arguments.description: STRONGER ANGULAR-DIAMETER ARGUMENT: The sun-moon apparent-angular-diameter ratio is constrained to ≈ 1.0 with extraordinary precision by the historical record of total eclipses (durations match predictions to milliseconds across millennia). The dome would need to show that its n(r) formula PRESERVES this ratio in all observed eclipse geometries — a strong constraint that quickly over-determines the n(r) coefficient. This is a quantitative constraint the dome can be challenged to demonstrate, replacing the current speculative 'might shrink → only annular' framing.

PER-STATION FSF SCALING IS THE STRONGEST WIN-LEVEL HOOK: WIN-025 explicitly uses a 'Firmament Scaling Function' to scale per-station eclipse magnetic effects across 9 observatories. That formula maps station-shadow-geometric distance to predicted dip amplitude. Under refraction, the shadow geometry that goes into this distance is wrong. The section currently mentions WIN-025 but doesn't dwell on the FSF mechanism — making the FSF the centerpiece of the per-station argument would land the differential-refraction critique directly on a WIN we have already verdict-classified as 'Std Model Explains' but which would also be 'Self-Contradicted' if the dome's own refraction fix were applied consistently. Two independent failure modes are stronger than one.

kernel_of_truth.description: The dome's recognition that aetheric refraction would shift apparent celestial positions is qualitatively correct physics — a refractive medium genuinely does shift apparent positions, and Snell's law applies universally to all light traversing the medium. The n(r) function is mathematically well-defined and produces specific computable values at every disc radius. If such a medium existed, differential refraction between objects at different positions in the cavity would be a real physical effect. The dome has shown intellectual honesty in flagging the sun's 5,733 km altitude as 'optical illusion due to aetheric refraction' rather than ignoring the sun/firmament collision. The section correctly identifies that this honesty creates a derivation gap (the moon's interaction with the same medium has not been computed).
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: Acknowledging refraction creates more problems than it solves. Selectively applying the medium to one body while claiming predictions that depend on consistent treatment of all light is the structural failure the section captures. However, the section's claim that the dome 'cannot compute' the geometry is slightly overstated — the dome could in principle compute it (with a great deal of effort), it just hasn't, and any such computation would either change the predicted values or expose the n(r) coefficient as further fitted-to-fit.

advocate_mode.best_defense: A fair-minded dome defender would mount three lines of defense: (1) The eclipse magnetic predictions (WIN-010, WIN-025, WIN-068) take observed per-station eclipse magnitudes from NASA catalogs as inputs and predict the resulting magnetic responses — they do not REQUIRE knowing the moon's physical altitude, because the input is the photon flux drop AT the observer, which is observationally fixed regardless of refraction. (2) The refractive index n(r) at the moon's orbital position (r ≈ 10,000 km, n ≈ 1.44) bends light, but for near-overhead observations the vertical light-path is through near-uniform n (radial-only function), so the per-observer apparent-position shift is small. (3) The angular-diameter critique cuts both ways — refraction could enlarge as easily as shrink, and the historical persistence of total eclipses constrains any net effect to be small.
advocate_mode.preemptive_recommendation: The section preempts (3) of these — defense line that 'refraction is sun-only' — but not (1) and (2). The decider should consider sharpening the section to address (1) by distinguishing single-station from multi-station WINs (only multi-station scaling formally requires shadow-cone geometry), and (2) by noting that the SHADOW PATH crosses the full n(r) range across the disc even when individual observers see modest local n. Without these additions, defense survives at score 4 — a strong adversary will land at least one of these three counters.

cross_win_consistency.inconsistencies_found: No cross-WIN inconsistencies in the SEC-1.9 arguments themselves. The eclipse WINs all share the same dependency on shadow geometry, and the differential-refraction critique applies consistently across them. Note: WIN-044 (Firmament Scaling Function) is already 'Self-Contradicted' for the firmament-collision reason; the differential-refraction argument adds an independent basis. WIN-039 (lunar magnetic) is listed in EXP-144's affected_wins but not named in the section prose — minor omission flagged in hole #6. The cross-SECTION inconsistency with Section 1.5 (hole #1) is the more serious consistency problem.

claims_checked: Section 1.9 claim: sun apparent 5,733 km vs physical < 4,200 km gap ≥ 1,500 km
Section 1.9 claim: 'comparable to glass' for n=1.44
Section 1.9 claim: dome model page lists no OPEN problem for moon-medium interaction
Cross-section consistency with Section 1.5 (n at equator) and Section 1.8 (n at equator)
quantitative_verification.computations: Python verification: At r=10,008 km, H(r) = 8537·exp(−10008/8619) = 8537·exp(−1.1612) = 2,674.4 km. n = 1 + 0.20·(8537/2674.4 − 1) = 1 + 0.20·2.193 = 1.439 ≈ 1.44 ✓. At r=20,015 km, H = 8537·exp(−20015/8619) = 8537·0.0982 = 838.4 km. n = 1 + 0.20·(8537/838.4 − 1) = 1 + 0.20·9.183 = 2.837 ≈ 2.84 ✓. Sun gap: 5,733 − 4,200 = 1,533 km — "at least 1,500 km" is correct. Glass typical n: crown 1.52, dense 1.62 — n=1.44 is comparable (slightly below typical glass; close to Lucite/PMMA at 1.49). 'Comparable to glass' is fair. Eclipse WIN count from data/wins.json: WIN-010, 011, 012, 013, 014, 020, 025, 044, 068 = 9 entries with 'eclipse' in claim/finding/detail text ✓. raw-text/05-model.txt OPEN list: OPEN-001, 003, 004, 006 (in progress); 002, 005 (resolved). No OPEN-007+. Section's claim 'no open problem addressing the moon's interaction with n(r)' is accurate ✓. CROSS-SECTION: Section 1.5 still contains 'at the equatorial ring (r = 14,105 km), n ≈ 1.83' — verified by string match. Section 1.5 ALSO contains '1.44 at the equator' later in its own prose. Section 1.8 contains '1.44 at the equator'. Section 1.9 contains 'r ≈ 10,008 km, n = 1.44'. Internal inconsistency confirmed.
quantitative_verification.discrepancies: All Section 1.9 numerical claims verify against the dome's published H(r) and the standard n(r) ansatz. The discrepancies are downstream — specifically the cross-section inconsistency with Section 1.5 (hole #1) and the internal-justification slip in EXP-144 (hole #7). Section 1.9 itself is numerically clean.

tldr_review.tldr_text: The dome's refraction fix shifts the sun's apparent position but never computes how the same medium shifts the moon. Since the sun and moon traverse different n(r) profiles, eclipse alignment — which requires physical overlap, not just optical coincidence — becomes indeterminate. The dome claims nine eclipse WINs from geometry it cannot compute.
tldr_review.issues: TLDR is broadly accurate but baldly asserts the disputed premise 'eclipse alignment requires physical overlap, not just optical coincidence' — see hole #8 and hole #2. A non-science reader may take this too literally; a science-literate reader will see it as begging the question. Severity minor — the underlying section argument can be tightened to support the TLDR, rather than weakening the TLDR.
tldr_review.recommendation: Optional: soften 'requires physical overlap' to 'depends on shadow geometry the dome's own refraction model leaves uncomputed.' Acceptable as-is if hole #2 is also addressed.

deeper_analysis: Section 1.9 is a substantive structural argument that survives most adversarial pressure. The core claim — that the dome's n(r) medium cannot be selectively scoped to the sun without contradicting Snell's law — is sound physics and well-defended. The numerical content is correctly derived from the dome's H(r) formula. The section's main weaknesses are not internal but contextual: (1) the legacy text in Section 1.5 contradicts Section 1.9's equator-r value, exposing an unfixed inconsistency the moment a reader follows our own cross-reference; (2) the section's strongest defender escape (apparent-magnitude inputs are observationally fixed, so per-station predictions don't need physical positions) is not preempted in the section text and lands the most likely adversary counter; (3) the radial-only structure of n(r) means the section's 'moonlight is bent' argument is most powerful for shadow-path calculations rather than per-observer position shifts. None of these holes individually invalidates the argument, but in combination they reduce the section from 'devastating' to 'strong'. The recommended action is minor edits (sharpen the per-station vs single-station distinction in eclipse WINs, soften the angular-diameter directional claim, add WIN-039 reference, issue a falsification challenge on the August 2026 prediction) plus a follow-up issue to patch Section 1.5's stale equator-r value. The verdict-impact claim ('no verdict changes') stands — this section adds structural argumentation, not new verdicts.

Dome sun apparent altitude 5,733 km — matches raw-text/05-model.txt line 38

citations_unchecked: 'Sun's apparent altitude shifted by at least 1,500 km' — algebraically 5,733 − 4,200 = 1,533 km, but this assumes the upper bound on physical sun altitude is exactly 4,200 km (the firmament limit at the polar region). The dome's text says '< 4,200 km' which means the gap could be larger; '1,500 km' is a minimum, and the section correctly says 'at least 1,500 km'.

follow_up_issues_recommended.title: Patch Section 1.5: replace stale 'r = 14,105 km, n ≈ 1.83' with corrected equator value (r ≈ 10,008 km, n = 1.44) — propagate ISS-855 fix that EXP-144 only applied to new SEC-1.9
follow_up_issues_recommended.blocks: Cross-section consistency between SEC-1.5 and SEC-1.8/1.9. Currently a defender finds contradictory equator-r values across our own adjacent sections.

follow_up_issues_recommended.title: Sharpen SEC-1.9 to address apparent-magnitude-sufficiency defense — distinguish single-station WINs (WIN-010) from multi-station scaling WINs (WIN-025, WIN-068) where shadow-cone geometry is unavoidable
follow_up_issues_recommended.blocks: Most likely adversary counter is unaddressed; defense survives at score 4.

follow_up_issues_recommended.title: Issue August 2026 falsification challenge — ask dome publicly whether its per-station eclipse magnetic predictions account for n(r) refraction, before the August 2026 test window
follow_up_issues_recommended.blocks: Converts structural critique into a binary public test — dome must either publish n(r) handling or admit the contradiction.
